revision.comment: Text replacement - "== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA ==" to "== AUTOGENERATED CONTENT FROM WIKIDATA == [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]]"
revision.text:
[[en:Aso Shrine]][[fi:Aso-jinja]][[fr:Aso-jinja]][[ja:阿蘇神社]][[ko:아소 신사]][[simple:Aso Shrine]][[zh:阿蘇神社]] {{short description|Shinto shrine in Kumamoto Prefecture, Japan|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Aso Shrine'''|阿蘇神社|''Aso-jinja''|qq=}} | native_name = 阿蘇神社 | image = Aso Jinja 01.jpg | caption = The Aso Shrine ''Rōmon'', an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}, pictured here in 2012, was destroyed in the {{ill|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|en|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|qid=Q23825118|lt=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|1=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes}}. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|32|56|52|N|131|06|57.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:30000|display=title,inline|qq=}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | native_name_lang = 日本語 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Shinto Shrine | deity = [[Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = 281 BC | date_destroyed = | location = 3083-1 {{ill|Ichinomiya, Kumamoto|en|Ichinomiya, Kumamoto|qid=Q5986245|lt=Ichinomiya-machi|1=Ichinomiya, Kumamoto}} Miyaji, {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso|1=Aso, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} 869-2612, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = http://asojinja.or.jp/ | architecture_style = Shinto shrine style | festival = | leadership = [[Aso clan]] (阿蘇家族） }} {{nihongo|'''Aso Shrine'''|阿蘇神社|''Aso-jinja''|qq=}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto Shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso|1=Aso, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.{{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and shrines,'' p. 477; Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/aso-jinja/ "Aso Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-29. Aso is one of the oldest {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s in Japan.{{cite web|website=[[Japan National Tourist Organization|JNTO]] |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/attractions/facilities/spots/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000ek1x.html|title=Aso-jinja Shrine|url-status=dead|archive-date=2017-10-15|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171015222732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/attractions/facilities/spots/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000ek1x.html|qq=}} This shrine holds several {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}, including ''Ichi-no-shinden'' (一の神殿), ''Ni-no-shinden'' (二の神殿), and ''Rōmon'' (楼門). The Aso family in charge of the shrine is said to have the second oldest recorded lineage in Japan after the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial|1=Imperial House of Japan}} family. The Aso Shrine was heavily damaged in the 2016 Kumamoto earthquakes. The shrine's ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (tower gate) completely collapsed. The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worshiping hall) also collapsed.{{cite news|url=http://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20160416/p2a/00m/0na/023000c|title=Designated national cultural asset at Aso Shrine collapses in Kumamoto quake|publisher=Mainichi Shimbun|date=2016-04-16|access-date=2016-04-16|qq=}} ==History== [[File:Tennō Jimmu.jpg|thumb|248x248px|Emperor Jimmu, who is said to be an ancestor of Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto.]] Aso Shrine at {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|qid=Q733710|lt=Mount Aso|1=Mount Aso}} in [[Kyushu]] is traditionally held to have been a center of worship before the accession of [[:en:Emperor Jinmu|Emperor Jinmu]]. The ''shikinaisha'' shrine complex at {{ill|Ichinomiya, Kumamoto|en|Ichinomiya, Kumamoto|qid=Q5986245|lt=Ichinomiya|1=Ichinomiya, Kumamoto}} in what is today {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} was said to have been established in 281 BC. The earliest records of the shrine are found in Chinese historical chronicles like the Zuisho-Wakoku-Den from the 07th century that states that the people of Aso held festivals every time Mt. Aso erupted to calm its wrath. Historical information of the shrine can also be found in the ''Nihon-Shoki, Nihon-Kiryaku, Shoku-Nihon-Kouki, Montoku-Jitsuroku, Sandai-Jitsuroku,'' and the ''Chikushi-no-Kuni-Fudoki-Itsubun''. Records also link the founding of the shrine to the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|Emperor Kōrei|qid=Q313041|lt=Emperor Kōrei|1=Emperor Kōrei}} (孝霊天皇, ''Kōrei-tennō''). By the middle of the 11th century, the shrine was involved in national issues as they played out across Kyushu. {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|qid=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}} ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC&dq=Aso+jinja&pg=PA86 ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon,'' p. 86.] During the ascendancy of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|qid=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}} exercised a significant influence over the affairs of Aso Shrine.Maas, Jeffrey P. (1976). [https://books.google.com/books?id=mi6sAAAAIAAJ&dq=Aso+shrine&pg=RA1-PA317 ''The Kamakura Bakufu: A Study in Documents,'' p. 317.] [[File:Nihonshoki jindai kan pages.jpg|left|thumb|Information about the shrine's existence can be found in the ''Nihon Shoki'' as well as several texts from China.]] The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto]] (健磐龍命), who was a grandson of Japan's first emperor and the brother of {{ill|Emperor Suizei|en|Emperor Suizei|qid=Q313321|lt=Emperor Suizei|1=Emperor Suizei}}, the second monarch on the traditional list of emperors.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 29. In the same period that {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} was establishing his palace at [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]] at the foot of {{ill|Yamato Sanzan|en|Yamato Sanzan|qid=Q1470150|lt=Mount Unebi|1=Yamato Sanzan}} in [[:en:Yamato province|Yamato province]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Imperial House,'' p. 28; [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/001/index.html ''Unebiyama-no Ushitora-no-sumi-no misasagi'' (''Unebi Goryō,'' tomb of Emperor Jimmu).] Takeiwatasu was sent to Aso where he helped establish a number of agricultural communities; and later, he is said to have built a palace at [[:en:Miyagi District, Miyagi|Miyagi]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''shrines,'' pp. 480-484. The original location of the shrine is uncertain because it was destroyed and rebuilt many times in or near the crater of Aso-san. The shrine is said to be thousands of years old, however the present buildings date only from the ''{{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|qid=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}}'' era (1830–1843).Ponsonby-Fane, ''shrines,'' p. 490. Construction of the shrine took 20 years from Tenpō 06 (1835) through {{ill|Kōka|en|Kōka|qid=Q1325317|lt=Koka|1=Kōka}} (1844-1848), {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|qid=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}} (1848-1854) and {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|qid=Q1327259|lt=Ansei|1=Ansei}} (1854-1860) periods. ''Aso'' was the chief Shinto shrine (''[[Ichinomiya]]'') of the old {{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|qid=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}. It serves today as one of the ''Ichinomiya'' of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 |qq=}}; retrieved 2012-10-29. From 1871 through 1946, the Aso Shrine was officially designated one of the ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines#Kanpei-sha|Kanpei-taisha]]'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Shinto belief== Aso-no-Ōkami, the ''[[kami]]'' or spirit who is believed to dwell at Aso Shrine has been worshipped from early times{{vague|date=November 2014|qq=}} as the guardian deity of safety in navigation, and today, Aso-no-Ōkami is seen as protecting worshippers from traffic accidents and other untoward events. [[Yabusame]] is an annual festival which brings together horse-mounted archers, special arrows and targets, and Shinto ritual at Aso Shrine.[http://edpas.net/journal/109/ Yabusame at Aso Shrine] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081204191951/http://edpas.net/journal/109/ |date=December 4, 2008 |qq=}} The ''Aso no Himatsuri'' festival has its origin in the month of March festivals such as ''Aso no Noyaki'' (the first burning in Aso) and ''Aso jinja no Hifuri shinji'' (the fire ritual of Aso Shrine).[http://www.gu.jal.com/world/en/guidetojapan/ JAL Guide to Japan:] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061026103601/http://www.gu.jal.com/world/en/guidetojapan/ |date=2006-10-26 |qq=}} [http://www.gu.jal.com/world/en/guidetojapan/city_information/fuk/ Fukuoaka]; {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061026165358/http://www.gu.jal.com/world/en/guidetojapan/city_information/fuk/ |date=2006-10-26 |qq=}} JNTO: [http://www.iexplore.com/dmap/Japan/Event/16312 Fire Festivals.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110607060650/http://www.iexplore.com/dmap/Japan/Event/16312 |date=June 7, 2011 |qq=}} Although rarely performed today, ceremonies which honor ancestors who settled near the {{ill|Aso Caldera|en|Aso Caldera|qid=Q11657618|lt=Aso caldera|1=Aso Caldera}} do continue to be associated with the Aso jinja.{{cite journal|last1=Akimoto |first1=Kazuhide|first2=Minako|last2=Nakamura |date=November 15, 2008 |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.3130/jaabe.7.209|title=Settlement Composition and Actual Condition of Grave Festivals of Aso Caldera Settlements |journal=Journal of Asian Architecture and Building Engineering|volume=7|issue=2|pages=209-216 |doi=10.3130/jaabe.7.209| doi-access=free|qq=}} === Deities === There are 13 [[kami]] enshrined at Aso shrine. [[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|The Goddess ''Amaterasu'' is said to be the source of ''Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto's'' divine powers, as her descendant.|259x259px]] The shrine's main kami is ''Ichinomiya Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto'' (一の宮健磐龍命) the creator of {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso|1=Aso, Kumamoto}} and the God credited with teaching the villagers about agriculture. He is said to be the descendant of Emperor Jinmu. Since Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto was descended from Emperor Jinmu who is said to have been human, legends surrounding his divinity is unclear. Some state that through his lineage from the Goddess [[Amaterasu]] he was given divine powers for creating Aso. Another legend states that the deity of the volcano, entered the body of Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto turning him from a human into a divine being. These are common folklore among the people with the only consensus being that over time, the deity of the land and the deity of the volcano was combined into one symbol by the name of ''Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto''. The second important deity is called ''Ninomiya Aso-tsu-hime-no-mikoto'' the wife of ''Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto'' (二の宮阿蘇都媛命). The third important deity mentioned is ''Juuichinomiya Kuni-no-miyatsuko-hayamikatama-no-mikoto'' (十一の宮園造速瓶玉命). The other deities are the kami of fire 火の神, the kami of water 水の神 flowing near the ''rōmon'' gate of the shrine and the other kami of agriculture 農の神. == Festivals == === U-no-matsuri === The Aso shrine's ''U-no-matsuri'' (卯の祭) is a 13-day event that occurs in March to welcome spring and pray for the beginning of the rice planting season. The U-no-matsuri marks the anniversary of Aso shrine's chief deity ''Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto's'' arrival in Aso. This event occurred in the second month of the lunar calendar which is March in the current calendar. The shrine's Guuji San perform sacred Shinto music and dances every day of the festival while praying for a rich harvest. Occurring simultaneously to the U-no-matsuri, is a prominent festival called the ''Tazukuri Matsuri'' (田作り祭り) which is celebrated over 7 days, and the ''Daihimonjiyaki'' (大火文字焼き) which is the lighting of the Chinese characters for "fire" and "flame" on the mountains which is also done around the same time and sometimes the same day as the Tazukuri Matsuri.{{Cite book|title=肥後一の宮阿蘇神社|last=末吉|first=駿一|publisher=(株）マインド|year=2018|pages=38, 39, 40 and 43|qq=}} === Dai-Himonjiyaki === [[File:Daihimonjiyaki.jpg|left|thumb|The ''Daihimonjiyaki'' ''大火文字焼き'' on Ojodake and Motozuka showing the kanji characters for 火 and 炎.]] The Daihimonjiyaki (大火文字焼き) is part of the ''Noyaki'' (のやき, burning of the hills) that occurs in Aso, at the beginning of March every year. The hills are burnt to stop the growth of trees allowing the grass to take over, thereby maintaining Aso's famous grasslands. The kanji (Chinese character) for fire ''Hi'' (火)、and the character for flame ''Hono'' (炎) is lit onto Ojo-dake and Motozuka (known locally as Kitazuka) which are two hills, on the second Saturday of March every year.{{Cite web|url=http://www.fukuoka-now.com/en/event/aso-dai-himonjiyaki/|title=Aso Dai Himonjiyaki|website=Fukuoka Now|language=ja|access-date=2019-04-18|qq=}} The symbol can be seen from the top of the caldera to the surrounding villages. Fireworks and market stalls are also planned as part of the overall festival. As of the year 2016, after the {{ill|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|en|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|qid=Q23825118|lt=Kumamoto earthquake|1=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes}}, this event has been discontinued by the city. === Tazukuri Matsuri === The Tazukuri Matsuri (田作り祭り) is a week-long festival held at Aso shrine starting from the ''Mi no hi'', (巳の日, the day of the snake) and continuing on to the ''I no hi'' (亥の, day of the boar). These days are according to the old Japanese calendar and respond to different days on the Gregorian calendar every year. The most notable days within this week is the Hifuri Shinji. The Tazukuri Matsuri is held in accordance with the belief that if the deities of the shrine got married before the rice planting season, the harvest would be rich. Each day of the matsuri corresponds to a wedding event celebrating the marriage of the deities. On day 1 (day of the snake) the male deity who will be the bridegroom and resides in Aso shrine is transferred to a mikoshi (portable shrine) by three Shinto priests and taken to the house of one of the priests of the shrine. On day 4 (day of the monkey) two of the shrine priests go to Yoshimatsu Shrine in Akamizu, Aso, to collect the "shintai" of the bride deity. The "shintai" is an object of worship believed to contain the spirit of a deity. They transfer the bride deity to a mikoshi where they return to Aso shrine. The villagers line up to light her path giving rise to the Hifuri Shinji festival. The last three days of the festival is considered the honeymoon of the deities at the shrine priests house. On the last day of the festival (day 7, day of the boar) seven shrine priests invite the deities to their house where they perform sacred music, rituals and dances. A sacred play is also performed to symbolize the growth of rice. ==== Hifuri Shinji ==== [[File:御前迎え 火振り横.jpg|thumb|264x264px|Swinging of the ''Hifuri'' at Aso Jinja, {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso City|1=Aso, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}.]] This is a fire-swinging ritual where bales of hay are lit on fire and swung around by the villagers to welcome the Bride deity to Aso shrine on the evening of her nuptials. This day falls on the fourth day of the Tazukuri Matsuri week, the ''Saru no hi'' (猿の日, day of the monkey). While the ceremony is conducted by the shrine's ''Guji San'' (priests) in the shrine itself, visitors and well-wishers celebrate by listening to taiko and participating in the fire swinging event. The day begins with two shinto priests from Aso shrine who go on horseback to collect the female deity from Yoshimatsu Shrine in Akamizu. The shrine priests collect the ''shintai'' of the bride deity from a sacred ''kashi'' (oak) tree on the shrine grounds. These branches are said to contain the spirit of the bride deity, who came from the heavens for her marriage. The "shintai" of the bride deity is placed into a ''mikoshi'' (portable shrine) to be transported. The procession for the bride starts in Akamizu but stops in eight shrines along the way. At each shrine the bride deity, receives gifts for her wedding. The procession can be viewed by the public. At around 6pm, the procession arrives at Aso shrine for the wedding ceremony to begin. In March, the sun usually sets before 6pm and so the villagers gather to light the way for the bride deity. The villagers set bales of hay (''hifuri'') on fire and swing them around to light up the area. The priests carry the bride deity into the shrine where the marriage ceremony takes place. After the ceremony, the couple deities are placed into the same mikoshi and transported to the house of a priest for their honeymoon. Outside, the villagers continue to light bales of hay on fire and swing them around to the accompaniment of taiko until around 8:30pm. === Onda Matsuri === [[File:阿蘇神社の御田祭.jpg|left|thumb|226x226px|The ''Unari''- women clad in white, walking in the ''gyouretsu'' procession in the fields of {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso City|1=Aso, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}.|alt=]] The Onda Matsuri is a Shinto festival held by Aso shrine to pray for a bountiful harvest of rice in the village of Aso. The matsuri involves the people of the village who join in the different factions needed to make up the procession. The procession, called a ''Gyouretsu'' (行列) includes the ''Unari'' (宇奈利, 14 women clad in white),{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.aso.kumamoto.jp/files/uploads/2015/05/Aso_4seasons_h1-4.pdf |title=Event Information |date=May 2015 |website=www.city.aso.kumamoto.jp |publisher=Aso Tourism Association |access-date=2019-04-23|qq=}} 4 ''mikoshi'' (神輿) containing the Gods, and farming dolls. The Gyouretsu set out from Aso shrine in the morning to walk around the surrounding rice fields where the shrine's Guji San pray for the Gods to bestow their blessings upon the young rice plants. The procession is accompanied by ''{{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|qid=Q221769|lt=Taiko|1=Taiko}}'' drums and a ceremonial song, sung by the men in the procession.{{Cite web|url=http://kumanago.jp/en/events/detail/430000003331.html|title=Onda Festival Kumamoto Nagomi Tourism Site|website=Kumamoto Nagomi Tourism Site|language=en|access-date=2019-04-24|qq=}} The Onda Matsuri is held on July 26 and 28 every year. The festivities on the 28th are open to the public and begins in the morning. On the morning of the festival the 12 Gods of Aso, the God of water (水の神) and the God of fire (火の神) are placed into 4 ''mikoshi'' (portable shrines) and carried on the shoulders of the men from the village. Following the mikoshi are the shrine priests on horseback as the second set in the lineup. The third set consists of the ''unari'' which are women from the village, clad in white and carrying the food for the Gods on their heads. The food consists of {{ill|Kombu|en|Kombu|qid=Q11261547|lt=konbu|1=Kombu}}, rice, and vegetables like eggplant but no meat.{{Cite web|url=http://kumamotojet.com/documents/YOKAs/2019SpringYOKA.pdf|title=2019 Spring YOKA|date=Spring 2019|website=Kumamoto JET|qq=}} Next in the lineup for the procession are young village boys who dress in white and carry dolls on wooden sticks with the faces of an old man and woman and an ox. This represents the old days when rice was planted manually before machinery came into use. The God of navigation (''Sarutahiko no Mikoto'') and the Representative of people who plant rice (''Saotome'') also join the procession. The procession then goes through the rice fields of the neighbouring village for the crops to receive the blessings of the Gods. === Tanomi Sai === [[File:阿蘇神社の田実祭.jpg|thumb|248x248px|''[[Yabusame]]'' at Aso ''Jinja'', {{ill|Aso, Kumamoto|en|Aso, Kumamoto|qid=Q733681|lt=Aso City|1=Aso, Kumamoto}}, {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}.|alt=]] The Tanomi Sai is the last of the three large Shinto festivals held by Aso Shrine for the year. The Guji San at Aso shrine conduct special ceremonies to give thanks to the Agriculture [[kami]] for the successful harvesting of the rice. As a sign of their gratitude to the gods, the farmers of the neighboring village give their first crop of rice as an offering. The festival is held on the 25th and 26th September every year, with the main events being held on the 25th. On the morning of the 25th of September, the farmers and Guji San gather at Aso shrine where they conduct the necessary rituals. Around midday, several tribute events are held on the shrine's 130m long sacred walking path.{{Cite web|url=https://ohmatsuri.com/en/matsuri-list/tanomi-sai-festival|title=Tanomi-sai Festival|last=Calendar|first=Japanese Traditional Festival|date=2019-04-24|website=ohmatsuri.com|language=en|access-date=2019-04-24|qq=}} The main event is the [[Yabusame]], which is Japanese archery (弓道, [[Kyūdō]]) performed on horseback. This event was initially performed by the men in the village, however these days, it is performed entirely by the equestrian club of the local Aso Chuo High School. The students of the club dress in clothing similar to the ones worn by the former Samurai family of Aso. The archers ride up and down the grounds trying their hand at sending an arrow into the center of the target. The event is not competition and is done solely for the entertainment of the Gods and villagers. {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}} matches are also held with members of the surrounding village's sumo club participating in the event. The 26th of September has no public events and is a day of prayer and rituals carried out by the shrine's priests. === Other festivals === The following festivals are held by the shrine annually but are not commonly observed by the public. Farmers and interested parties are welcome to observe: ==== Toukano-sechie ==== The Toukano-sechie is held on January 13 of the lunar calendar (mid-February now) and is a ceremony where farmers sing "''Tauta''" -rice planting songs while working. The lyrics celebrate the coming New Year. Traditionally people stamp while singing the song to wake up the earth spirits.{{Cite book|last1=Takano|first1=Tomoaki|title=History and Festivals of the Aso Shrine|last2=Uchimura|first2=Hiroaki|publisher=Aso Shrine|year=2006|location=Aso Shrine, Ichinomiya, Aso City.|qq=}} ==== Kaza-matsuri ==== The Kaza-matsuri is held twice a year; April 4th of the lunar calendar (mid-May now) and July 04th (mid August now). The festival is held to pray for the protection of the crops from wind damage. At this time of the year, winds are especially strong in Aso which leads to crop damage. The Shrine's Guuji San exorcise the winds by utilizing ''Gohei'' (a ritual for driving away evil winds). The Guuji San drive the winds from Kaza-miya, a shrine which enshrines the deities of the Wind in {{ill|Miyaji Station|en|Miyaji Station|qid=Q6884211|lt=Miyaji|1=Miyaji Station}}, Aso, to the one in Teno. The Guuji San follow two different routes to the same destination. ==== Nemurinagashi ==== The Nemurinagashi is celebrated every year on August 06. This date corresponds to the mid-summer day where hotter temperatures bring general fatigue on the farmers. On the night of the 06th, the farmers walk around Aso village singing Tauta songs for the last time, in their efforts to banish sleep . ==== Hitaki Shinji ==== The Guuji San of Aso shrine participate in a ritual at Shimo Shrine from August 19th, to October 19th every year. A young girl from the village is chosen as the "Lighting girl" who conducts the prayer for frost free weather to protect the crops from the curse of ''Kihachi''. The wood is kept burning in a ''hitaki'' (woodburning den) for 59 days. The origins of the festivals can be traced back 700 years from the ''Aso-jinja-shinnoubutsu-chumon-utsusi'', an old book detailing the chronicles of Aso shrine. == Mythology of the shrine == === The creation of Aso === [[File:Ojodake.jpg|thumb|Ojodake on Mt. Aso where ''Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto'' practised his archery.]] According to an interview with several priests at Aso shrine, the legend of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto and the formation of Aso City, is as follows: Thousands of years ago, the crater now known as Aso city was said to be filled with water and was thereby a huge lake surrounded by the outer rim of the caldera. Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto kicked in the western side of the caldera at what is now known as {{ill|Tateno Station (Kumamoto)|en|Tateno Station (Kumamoto)|qid=Q7688002|lt=Tateno|1=Tateno Station (Kumamoto)}}, {{ill|Minamiaso, Kumamoto|en|Minamiaso, Kumamoto|qid=Q1184681|lt=Minami-Aso|1=Minamiaso, Kumamoto}}. He fell from the effort and was unable to stand for a while hence the name "Tateno" meaning "''unable to stand up''."As the water drained away forming rivers on the outside of the valley, a huge catfish (''[[Namazu|Namazu]])'' appeared. The catfish was settled at the bottom of the lake where some stories say it was asked to leave by Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto. The catfish went to Namazu, {{ill|Kashima, Kumamoto|en|Kashima, Kumamoto|qid=Q1184801|lt=Kashima|1=Kashima, Kumamoto}} where Namazu-sha Shrine was established to pay homage to the soul of the catfish as the spirit of the lake. Now the shrine is in the precincts of the Kokuzo Shrine.{{Cite news|url=http://www.aso-geopark.jp/|title=The deity of the Volcano Geosite|date=2014|work=Aso GeoPark Geosite Map|publisher=Aso Local Development Design Centre Foundation|type=Guide Map|qq=}} [[File:Matoishi.jpg|thumb|The ''Matoishi'' (target stone) that Kihachi was sent to, to retrieve the arrows for Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto.]] === The legend of Kihachi === The legend of Kihachi surrounds the Hitaki-Shinji festival commemorated in Aso annually. Legend states that Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto liked practicing his archery. He would stand on Ojodake (a cone shaped formation on Mt. Aso) and practice shooting ''matoishi'' (target stone) in what is now Matoishi in Aso, located north of the caldera. Kihachi his servant, was in charge of bringing the arrows back from the ''matoishi'' to Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto. After the 100th arrow was shot, Kihachi got tired and kicked the arrow back breaking it in half. Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto was furious and drawing his sword slayed Kihachi cutting off his head. Kihachi's head ascended as a deity vowing to exact revenge on Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto's village of Aso. Later, during the winter months it frosted in Aso valley damaging all of the crops. Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto realised that this was the revenge that Kihachi had spoken of and so he enshrined his soul at ''Shimo Shrine'' (''frost shrine'') to abate his anger. A ritual called the Hitaki-Shinji was started where a fire is kept for 2 months to pray for a good harvest and prevent frost damage to the crops. They say that this is why the winters in Aso are so cold. === Takasago-no-matsu === There is a sacred marriage pine on the grounds of Aso shrine. It is said that if a woman walks around the tree twice counterclockwise and the man she loves walks around the tree once clockwise he would encompass her wish and the two will have a happy relationship. === Aso Ogami === The Aso Ogami is a kami stone with the divine powers to grant wishes. It is said that the Ogami put all his wishes into the stone and in turn grants them to anyone who makes a wish by keeping their wish in their mind, then stroking the stone three times and then reciting their wish. == Chief Priest == The position of Chief Priest of Aso Shrine is hereditary and is passed down from generation to generation with the oldest male in the family becoming the new head of the shrine. The Aso family has the second oldest recorded lineage in Japan after the Imperial family. The family themselves are said to be descendants of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto. The Aso family were priests but also rose to power as a powerful samurai clan in the 11th century. They remained in prominence until the 16th century as their feudal lord continued to rule over half of what is now known as Kumamoto Prefecture. This contributed economically to the family and the shrine, allowing them to rebuild shrine buildings and hold festivals. It also made it possible for them to establish branches of the Aso shrine in other locations. Some of those shrines still remain in up to 500 locations to this day. Koreyuki Aso (''Aso Koreyuki in Japanese name order'') was the 91st Chief Priest of Aso Shrine with his son being the 92nd. {{Tree chart/start|align=center|summary=Aso family tree|qq=}} {{Tree chart| | | | | |EmJ|EmJ={{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}||qq=}} {{Tree chart| | |,|-|-|-|+|-|-|-|.| |qq=}} {{Tree chart| |MOM||DAD| |DAISY|y| Lee|MOM=Kanakori no Kami |DAD=[[Kamuyaimimi|Kamuyaimimi no Mikoto]]|DAISY=Kunitatsu no Kami |Lee=Hime Miko no Kami||qq=}} {{Tree chart| ||!| | | |!| | | | ,| -|^|- |.||qq=}} {{Tree chart| |Koutou | | ME |y | SIS | |SHIN|y|YAYA| |Koutou=Imperial Family Line|ME=Takeiwatatsu no Mikoto|SIS=Asotsuhime no Mikoto|SHIN=Shinhiko no Mikoto|YAYA=Yahime no Kami ||qq=}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| ,| -|^|- |.||qq=}} {{Tree chart| || | | | | |MOM| | | | DAD|| DAISY |MOM=Hayamikatama no Mikoto|DAD=Wakahiko no kami|DAISY=Shinhime no Mikoto|qq=}} {{Tree chart| ||||| | | |!| | | ||| !| |qq=}} {{Tree chart| |||MOM| y| DAD|| | | DAISY |MOM=Wakahime no Kami|DAD=Hikomiko no Kami|DAISY=Aso Shrine Extended Family|qq=}} {{Tree chart| ||| | ||!||qq=}} {{Tree chart| | | | | | MOM|MOM=''Current'' Aso Shrine Priests|qq=}} {{Tree chart/end|qq=}} == Structure == Aso shrine is said to have been built 2300 years ago in a time where shrines were torn down and moved to more divine locations. The shrine also suffered from fires and earthquakes making most locations temporary. A permanent location wasn't chosen until 281 AD. The shrine has since been burnt down and damaged in earthquakes causing the current building to only date back to the 1830s. Most recently the shrine suffered considerable damage during the {{ill|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|en|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|qid=Q23825118|lt=2016 Kumamoto Earthquake|1=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes}} and is currently under reconstruction. === External area of the shrine === ==== Shrine grounds ==== [[File:The sideways approach of Aso shrine..jpg|left|thumb|The "''sideways approach''" of Aso shrine that connects Mt. Nakadake to Aso Shrine and Kokuzo shrine. ]] There are three entrances to the shrine with the south gate (''minami [[torii]]'') facing Mount Aso, and the north gate leading onto the shopping street of ''Nakadori, Ichinomiya Aso''. A guide office is situated by the main entrance. There are also two car parks, the first being by the eastern entrance of the shrine, and another by the north gate. The tombs of the ''ichi-no-shinden''(一の神殿) is on the right side of the eastern entrance and the tomb of the ''ni-no-shinden''(二の神殿) is on the left. The ''mizu-no-kami''(水の神) is to the side of the eastern entrance of the shrine grounds.{{Cite book|last=Honda|first=Fujio|title=阿蘇神社|publisher=Gakken Publishing Company|year=2019|language=ja|qq=}} The entrance to the general shrine is unique in that the approach to the shrine extends sideways. This was done to align Mt. Nakadake which is the only peak in Mt. Aso to have a crater with Aso Shrine and Kokuzo Shrine. The approach was made sideways to welcome the deity of the volcano into the shrine. The grounds surrounding the shrine contain multiple {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|qid=Q871991|lt=Cherry blossom|1=Cherry blossom}} trees, {{ill|Ginkgo biloba|en|Ginkgo biloba|qid=Q43284|lt=ginkgo|1=Ginkgo biloba}} trees and {{ill|Acer palmatum|en|Acer palmatum|qid=Q269224|lt=momiji|1=Acer palmatum}} trees, ensuring that the shrine's exterior displays the four seasons. ==== Shrine building ==== The exterior of the shrine facing the east has three gates. The main gate is the Rōmon (楼門 tower gate) which is 18m tall and has a double roof which is rare in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} architecture. It was erected in the late Edo period 1850 (嘉永3rd year ). It is flanked on both sides by two smaller gates called the Kangyomon (還御門) and Kamikomon (神幸門)both built in 1849 (嘉永2nd year ). These are usually closed but are opened during the ''Onda Matsuri'' to allow the 4 portable shrines to exit the main shrine. This is done in accordance with the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]] System. === Internal area of the shrine === The ''haiden'' (hall of worship) was built in 1948. it is directly on the path from the rōmon to the inside of the shrine. At the side of this path is a Takasago Pine tree grown from a pine nut brought from Onoe in ''Harima-kuni'' (now known as {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}) in 1060 and planted. To the side of that is the Aso Ogami wishing stone. The shrine's office of internal affairs is to the left of the haiden. ==== Restricted area ==== Behind the haiden in Aso shrine are the three smaller shrines said to house Takeiwatatsu-no-mikoto and the other 11 kami of Aso shrine. These are the ''ichi-no-shinden'' built in 天保11th year (1840), the ''ni-no-shinden'' built in 天保 13 年(1842), and the ''san-no-shinden'' built in 天保 14th year (1843). == 2016 earthquakes == [[File:Aso Shrine Tower Gate.jpg|left|thumb|245x245px|The collapsed state of the ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (tower) gate in Aso shrine. Repairs are expected to be completed in 2023.]] [[File:Aso Shrine Construction Work.jpg|thumb|Construction work being done on Aso shrine. The shrine was damaged in the {{ill|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|en|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|qid=Q23825118|lt=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|1=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes}}.]] Aso Shrine was heavily damaged during the {{ill|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|en|2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|qid=Q23825118|lt=2016 earthquakes|1=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes}} that struck Kumamoto on April 16, 2016, at 1:25 am {{ill|Japan Standard Time|en|Japan Standard Time|qid=Q909085|lt=JST|1=Japan Standard Time}}, at a magnitude of 7.0. The shrine's ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (tower gate) was completely destroyed as well as the ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall).{{Citation|title=2016 Kumamoto earthquakes|date=2020-02-01|url=https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2016_Kumamoto_earthquakes&oldid=938619899|work=Wikipedia|language=en|access-date=2020-02-14|qq=}} The smaller Kangyomon and Kamikomon gates were also partially destroyed along with several smaller shrines said to house the deities on the inner parts of the main shrine. Reconstruction efforts are already underway with several smaller structures already having been completely repaired. Final reconstruction efforts are projected to be completed sometime in 2023. With the collapse of the rōmon gate, the entrance to the shrine is now situated near the south gate. A temporary building used for goshuin and omikuji are situated in the inner area of the rōmon gate. List of damaged properties in Aso shrine and their projected repair dates:
{| class="wikitable" |+List of damaged properties in Aso shrine and the projected dates of Completion for total repair. The properties are listed in order of importance to the public. !Structures !Construction date (beginning) !Construction date (end) |- |''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (tower gate) |November 2016 |Anticipated date: 2023 |- |''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' (worship hall) |November 2016 |August 2018 |- |''Kangyomon and Kamikomon'' (smaller gates) |May 2017 |September 2018 |- |''Ichi no Shinden'' (shrine house of the kami) |July 2018 |March 2019 |- |''Ni no Shinden'' (shrine house of the kami) |May 2017 |March 2019 |- |''San no Shinden'' (shrine house of the kami) |December 2016 |September 2018 |}
== Related shrines == Other Aso shrines include: {{dynamic list|qq=}} * Aso-jinja in {{ill|Hamura, Tokyo|en|Hamura, Tokyo|qid=Q270465|lt=Hamura, Tokyo|1=Hamura, Tokyo}}.[http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:IshiDoro0149.jpg Fg2 photo description] *{{ill|Aoi Aso Shrine|en|Aoi Aso Shrine|qid=Q2857960|lt=Aoi Aso Shrine|1=Aoi Aso Shrine}} in {{ill|Hitoyoshi, Kumamoto|en|Hitoyoshi, Kumamoto|qid=Q861292|lt=Hitoyoshi, Kumamoto|1=Hitoyoshi, Kumamoto}}.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aoisan.jp/top_kami.html|title=青井阿蘇神社＜神＞|website=www.aoisan.jp|access-date=2019-04-19|qq=}} *461 shrines in {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}}. *32 shrines in {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. *7 shrines in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. *4 shrines in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. *19 shrines in [[Honshu]]. ''This list from Aoi Aso Shrine to Honshu is cited here.'' ==See also== {{Portal|Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2|qq=}} .]] ==References== * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|qid=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii and Shôichirô Yoshida. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=NBlDYE38ODYC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1575-1|qq=}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/title/51096469 OCLC 51096469] * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. It is located in [[Higo Province]]. == Shikinaisha (式内社) == {| class="wikitable sortable" ! District !! Name !! Funding category !! Rank !! Notes !! Same as !! Co-ords !!
